commentBody: If you do the Midi first, you get 4A for free in the main...

I'm happy to see flowers in May, and having the B E E S mark where they CROSSPOLINATED was fun. Nice debut, James. 
Now, editors, about the Monday-level clues...
Felt like a long Monday. Cute theme, but easy.
"It’s a very good example of what I would consider to be a perfect Wednesday..." Isaac, How about a perfect Monday? https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-05-06

HOLYTOLEDO BATMAN, that puzzle was right in the middle of my wheelhouse.  I’ll try to avoid using any flowery prose to describe it.  I thought it was a pleasant Wednesday exercise with some ok clues and a theme near and dear to my heart.  As an avid gardener I appreciated the floral entries.  My own garden contains  all the flowers mentioned except for HIBISCUS and DAISY.  When I moved into my current home 34 years ago, I wasn’t much of a gardener, and there wasn’t much of a garden here.  But when fall arrived a trio of deep purple ASTERs bloomed along the front walk and I was hooked. After 34 years of planting and tending what became a sort of cottage garden, I am beginning to dread the day I will no longer be able to care for it myself.

I did not see these clues, for the most part, as Monday-easy, but then again my difficulty detector is on the fritz.  But [Scary spice, by another nickname] was something I was only going to get it I knew every cross.  

Also, I don't know flowers at all.  

I honestly think that by trying to do archive puzzles from the 00's, that I've ruined my self-confidence.  There are so many that I don't know, or don't know if I know, that I kind of freeze.  A lot of it is because they're harder, but some of it is because they aren't as fresh.  For example, [Continental, for example] was LINCOLN.  It's been a long, long time since I've seen a Lincoln Continental.

I don't know if this is a phase new solvers sometimes go through, or if it's a malady of my own making.

But it's making me grumpy.

Fun puzzle, but tough by Wednesday standards.

Ironically even though I'm not up on pop culture I might have done better with the pop culture clues, I know names of a lot of flowers but I don't know much about the details.  I'm halfway between being glad they didn't just go with pop culture and wishing they didn't put so many crossing clues in a single knowledge set.  I know that PEONY is a flower but don't know what season it blossoms in.  I kinda wish they went more half and half there.

A bunch of really good clues like HOLY TOLEDO, CROSS POLINATED, etc.

NE and NW were the trouble areas for me.  Besides not knowing the botanical details of specific flowers, I do not understand ELAN as an answer for "Stylish", and a bunch of other words in those areas were obscure enough to be tough to get without the flowers.

For me at least, a Wednesday that took longer than my Friday average.

OCCAM’s utility knife: the garden tool you are seeking is not in the shed, but out there somewhere, having spent the winter outdoors.

OCCAM’s seed packet: the melon seeds you saved last year cross pollinated and will yield tasteless fruit.

OCCAM’s wheelbarrow: it’s too full. Stop now before you throw out your back again.

Happy gardening season, everyone!

Pros: Not many American sports, actors, towns,  colleges, brands etc - hurray! I got HAMM thanks to the Midi today, so that was a bonus, and guessed the Stax city with _EM____ filled in. The flowers were a nice addition. I'm like the columnist: not a particularly avid gardener/florist, but enough to get the entries without any trouble. Plenty of nice clues and fill apart from those too.
Con: didn't really need the theme during the solve. As the columnist said it was more like a first-of-the-month one for me, just a normal xword with an unusual number of entries on a particular topic. I much prefer a theme you can really get into and make use of.
Always nice to see my adopted country of Peru in here - we do get within just a few kms of the equator but not quite! The Putumayo river border with Colombia just bends away from it at the last moment. Northernmost coordinates are 0°02'19" South (!) 75°10'55" West. 
Overall an enjoyable puzzle, which is the main thing. Thanks James, and congrats on the debut!

My history with the NYT crossword goes back about 6 years or so.  So I’m experienced, but not a vet who reminisces about the good old days in these puzzles.  So for Barry, it might have been Monday level easy but, for me (during my time solving these puzzles), it seemed to be a spot on Wednesday…. which is refreshing since the puzzles of 2026 (and perhaps a bit before) have have been easier than my 6-year average expectation.

Hi all, how are we doing? Living the dream? Stoking the fires? Feeling your age? Minding the roost? 

Having gotten the H for the clue ["Great Scott!"] I should've known better than to put down HIGHLANDER, but honestly, can you think of a great Scott whose name begins with an H? But the quote marks were the giveaway. Connor Mac Leod (Christopher Lambert) -- he was a he11a Scotsman. 

Started reading up on hybridization by bees & whether all the flowers in the puzzle are hybrids rather than species flowers. But all the flowers here come as both species, hybrids, and cultivars, so it's not that easy to say. None of the flowers is a straight-up mutt, there's no easy distinction. You can mainly tell by their Latin names where an "x" indicates a hybrid. I can see Socrates working on me: "So what then is a hybrid? Can a cultivar be a hybrid? Are you not then a hybrid? If two different species plants produce a species plant, is God a hybrid? Or a species?"

I learned hybridization can only take place if the two varieties are related enough, and many plants evolved barriers toward preventing that kind of funny business, such as petal shape, different bloom times, etc.  Take that, busy bees! 

I was hoping the bees in this puzzle would produce some new hybrids: the PEOBISCUS, PRIM BELL, VIOLASTER, DAIZALEA, and my favorite triple hybrid, the BLUE AST ROSE. But it was not to bee. 

Good debut. It was DEARLY BEE-LOVIN'

I can never hear or read "edelweiss" —as in the clue for ASTER [cousin of edelweiss] —and not think of this song, which is one of my two favorites in "The Sound of Music" – https://youtu.be/z6-P3pFhmQI?si=W5CTrtaUVFgyE30B So now here I am, listening to this and feeling like I've just been handed a huge bouquet of flowers to start May off right. Thank you, James! You've made my day! (And the puzzle, in totum, was a fine debut. Congratulations!) When I was in my teens, someone said to me that flowers that are given with love last the longest. Old wives' tale or not, the notion has served to fill my heart when, for example, I've received a rose that bloomed and seemed to mummify, refusing to droop, and peonies that opened up in slow motion, each day another bit, and then went on and on and on... Marilyn insisted that Diamonds Are a Girl's Best Friend, but what can I say, I think flowers are... P.S. SOCRATIC crossing AGORA was a special touch and didn't go unnoticed. Kudos, James!

Anyone who has ever complained about too many sports clues in a CW can take an upper deck seat courtesy of a RESALE. This was brutal. But, my flower knowledge just gained a few ticks. At least it wasn't another  Wicked theme. Cheers!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Impressive debut.

How mind-blowing would it have been if the specific crossing flower pairs were, in fact, crosspollinatable?

(Knowing nothing about botany, I checked.  And they're not.)

Would have been not only a strong rookie outing but a never-before-seen level of meta-verisimilitude in a Times grid, I'd wager.

A fragrant puzzle, as promised by those April showers. Relaxing, engaging, encouraging, just right for a Wednesday.

A sure touch, James Mattina, and delicate, the balance for a tiptop puzzle.  Thank you.

I was happy to see William Bolcom mentioned in 3D. In addition to his set of etudes, he wrote a wonderful collection of piano rags. Here's a video of pianist Marc-André Hamelin playing Bolcom's lovely "Graceful Ghost" rag: - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zCTLr0j6Gs0

Beautiful puzzle

Refreshing theme, learned some interesting fun facts (azaleas!!)

And 'Bees' at the crosspollination points - nice...

Congrats on landing this very solid debut!
Love the botanical theme, but I don't think the HIBISCUS is as strongly associated with lei-making as pikake, tiaré, orchid, and plumeria. It's more likely a flower behind the ear.

My solve time was well below my average, but I still agree with Isaac that this wonderful debut puzzle by James Martina is a perfect Wednesday. Maybe it’s because I just got such a kick out of James’ constructor notes. Do I smell freesia???

I am newbie here and I almost had three (count 'em three) days in a row without any lookups. But I got caught up on 6D [Bed cover]--which I should know because I was MULCHing today and yesterday, etc.

Today I was cutting up my spent daffodils which are taking over and which I removed. I saved the gazillion bulbs and cut the stems up in little pieces and threw them in the flower bed as MULCH. Constantly building the topsoil up with mulch is popular advice for today's gardeners, (rather than turning over the soil to add biomass--which will disturb the complex micro biome there. [Is that a word?])

In my garden I have PEONies, ASTERs, HIBISCUS (also called Rose of Sharon), AZALEA, TOMATO, and SAGE. Another nice spring flower just coming into bloom: lilac. Oh, and don't forget iris.
Time was about midway between my Tuesday and Wednesday averages. I liked the theme and it actually helped me complete the puzzle.
I really liked it. It felt like something of a throwback.

Is there a reason the various NYT crosswords share so many words and clues? I've been noticing many of the same answers lately, often only a day or two apart.

Today the Midi and the Crossword have clues referencing the same 90s soccer player, 21 Across and 4 Across respectively, and worse, each clue has the answer to the other.

I guess some people might get a kick out noticing that, but I don't. It just feels lazy, and often improbable given the vast number of possible clues. Like writers are copying each other.

I had fun. For me the clues seemed appropriately Wednesday-ish and thinking was required to solve. And it was a fun theme, which brought to mind this Talking Heads tune. https://youtu.be/2twY8YQYDBE?si=WAFHZvVuTf_zXuWr Congratulations on the debut.

I'm hopeless with flowers - in Polish I know many of their names, but I actually only recognize a few of them in the real world: probably the ones that grew in my grandmothers allotment in my formative years. When I think "aster", for example (the Polish and English name is the same) no image forms in my head, and I can think of none of its characteristics. In English it's worse - I basically only know the names of flowers similar to Polish ones (róża/rose, tulipan/tulip, peonia/peony etc.). I have no idea what a bluebell is, for example, or a primrose. So yeah, I needed *loads* of crosses to get today's themed entries. The clues meant nothing to me, and I had to dredge many of the flower names from the deepest recesses of my mind. 

The NW corner was the last to fall for me, as I obviously didn't know the answer to the musical clue straight away, and I have mostly no idea about how zodiac signs align with the calendar, so the LYBRA clue was a huge mystery until I got many crosses there.

Given all my difficulties, I was surprised to finish well below my Wednesday average.

The bees’ knees as we used to say (or maybe my parents at one time). Nice debut and I thought decently crunchy for a Wednesday, personally.

Where’s Mike tonight? There’s too many bee puns to even pick from, but I won’t try to steal his thunder

"How'd you do on the botany test?"
"Great! I knew all the anthers."

("Sounds like you're pollen through!")

I had to do forensics after the solve to figure out the theme.  Fun puzzle.  

I don’t know nothing about grids and black squares. But it seems like there are very few blacks for a Wednesday grid?  I keep turning and squinting, but I can’t get my eyes to make a magic  eye flower or bee.  Maybe a butterfly flying northeast?

Congrats on the debut. James
Ockham.

I loved this puzzle, James ! As a Texan, I must say, we love our Blue Bell Creamery and its contented cows from "cow heaven," Brenham, Texas. When I was a Baylor University student in the '70s, Blue Bell was not yet widely distributed. We could only buy it on the black market, which in Waco was a Frigidaire freezer in the back of the garage of a gas station. I still believe the black market Blue Bell was even creamier and more delicious than grocery store Blue Bell. Happy Wildflower Season, Texas !

I spent last Saturday planting a bunch of flowers, including a patch of daisies. Simple yet cheerful, they are my favorite flowers because they always look the happiest. I put them next to the balloon flowers, my perennial drama queens who make a big show of puffing out their heads before bursting into bloom.

Both of them stay away from the azaleas, who are nothing but trouble.
The reveal had me buzzing!
I don't think I've ever commented on a Wednesday puzzle before but had to make an exception for this utterly delightful and satisfying debut.  Looking forward to many more, Mr. Mattina.

I especially like the theme clues, particularly [Flower found at the end of a rainbow] for VIOLET, [Flower found along a metaphorical path of ease] for PRIMROSE, [Bloom whose toxic nectar is one source of "mad honey”] for AZALEA and [“Link" in a botanical chain] for DAISY. 

Other than that the clues were disappointedly straightforward. The only thing close to a misdirect was [Bed cover].

[DC good guy], sadly, cannot possibly be  a misdirect. 

A pleasant puzzle and a rosy theme. Congratulations on your debut, James.
Terribly created puzzle. I never comment on these but every once in a while, someone who’s not good at creating these puzzles gets the opportunity to do so!
I rather enjoyed it. Just challenging enough to make beefing a legit possibility. I'm running an insanely high streak now, and I know I'll eventually beef, but please, God, not on a Wednesday!
If you're looking for a more challenging but definitely doable Wednesday, I just (after one alphabet run) finished this one in the archives. https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/1995/05/17

Congratulations on an enjoyable debut, Mr. Mattina!

Two answers in particular reminded me of my 50+ years (gulp!) of living in Texas:  17A BLUE BELL ice cream (I regret we never made it to their creamery in Brenham) and 59A SOCRATIC method (a few of my professors at the UT Law School were adept at using that method, and their classes were ones I looked forward to). 

30D brought back memories of a Trivial Pursuit game with my brother and his family in Dallas; it seemed that no matter what the question was that night, the answer was PERU. (One of my nephews was then in middle school and learning Spanish, so we practiced pronouncing it the way a native Peruvian might.)

On the other hand, 20A CBD is right out of my new home. There are dispensaries all over town.

Hope we see you back soon! Thanks for the fun!

This was a slow solve for me, mostly because of the current lack of caffeine in my system.  
Satisfyingly rigorous, happy to see this level of difficulty on a Wednesday.

I usually rush to the comments to kvetch when a puzzle is a flop (cough…Sunday…), so it feels nice to start my day by giving some positive feedback.

😊

Hope to see more puzzles from this constructor soon!

Wow! Great puzzle! Second day in a row in which I've found the puzzle trickier than usual for that day--the NE was a particular blank, from which only the Dark Knight could save me (and I know virtually nothing about comics.) 3D seemed a bizarrely specific way to clue cross-word staple ETUDE--was that your own clue, Mr. Mattina? Ann Arbor, MI, resident William Bolcom won the Pulitzer prize in 1988, and although I could deduce ETUDE easily enough from the crosses, my mind first jumped to RAG, as that is the musical form this protean composer is most closely associated with. Here is his "big hit," (a relative term), the "Graceful Ghost Rag": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zCTLr0j6Gs0&list=RDzCTLr0j6Gs0&start_radio=1 Also in A2, the University of Michigan's Nichols Arboretum features an extensive collection of PEONEs, which is just coming into bloom now: https://mbgna.umich.edu/whats-bloom-peony-garden TOLEDO also has a quite fine Botanical Garden, as well, and features good collections of hostas and daylilies. (Credit goes to @Mr. Connell for introducing me to the TBG.)
Excellent puzzle while noting that it mat not be possible to come up with a more obscure clue than one that asks for the alternate nickname of a Spice Girl. Come on.
Florals for spring? Groundbreaking. https://youtu.be/a6MG--mTDZQ?si=R8QA_aYF9tgKzB08 That's all.
James Mattina, what lovely sprightly constructor notes  - I hope the trees are in full gorgeous bloom today in Montreal too 😊

Today's puzzle involving flower names reminded me of an incident from long ago, and a related one from just a little while ago.

I went to a liberal arts college that had a required science component.  For my last science class, knowing that I wasn't interested in being a science major, I took Plant Tax[onomy].  Its required lab was taking walks in the Woods that abutted our beautiful campus.  How great is that!

Now, almost 50 years later, whenever I go for a walk in the woods, I remember something that I learned in that class, the name of a flower or a tree or a weed, its tell-tale features.  ("Look!  A spring beauty! [Claytonia virginica] It only has two sepals.")

Just a few years ago, I was able to track down the professor who taught that class and thank him for the way his teaching had enriched my life.
I was lucky to have contacted him.  He was in his late 80s; and he died shortly thereafter.  A colleague of his reached out to me after his death to tell me how much my letter meant to him and to ask if he could use passages from my letter in his obituary.

A lesson to us all.  Gratitude is important.  Give thanks while you still can.
Congratulations, Mr. Mattina on a lovely debut!
Thanks for a lovely start to the day — doing this puzzle while looking out the window to admire my rhododendrons.
A fun puzzle, even for someone who's not particularly strong on flower names (or spellings). And always great to see a budding constructor hit the big time.

Really cool puzzle, with thorns! Solved a little over my Weds average due to my lack of flower knowledge and more than enough clever misdirection to keep it challenging. Still, it was totally solvable using the crosses and hopefully I learned something. Nice to see Vientiane in the clue, I’ve never been there but I mention it in one of my songs.

OMG!!  I just started my solve--uncharacteristically, at the top--and I reached 4D "Blossom in a lei".....and I ...Could. Not. Believe. My. Eyes!!!
Is there no end to ignorance?  I would love to watch someone string the one-day blooms of the HIBISCUS to make a lei......just to see if there IS, in fact, a limit to what Art Buchwald labeled "Dumbth."
Plumeria, yes.  Pikake, yes.  Carnations were the big deal in the Fifties when my sister had a date to the prom.  Long-lasting, fragrant, amenable to being strung as a LEI.  
Never, never, never HIBISCUS.
I am going to get a cold cloth for my head so I can do the rest of the puzzle.  But that clue??? Oh god ohgod ohgod.
Really enjoyed this puzzle, wonderful debut!!

Nice puzzle. Typical long Wednesday workout for me, but a really nice moment when I finally tumbled to the theme. Don't recall another one quite like this. And... ... puzzle find today - a Tuesday from August 1, 2017 by Jay Kaskel. This one was all in the clues. Some examples: "Shocking!" to an astronomer? OHMYSTARS And all the other theme clues started with "Shocking?" Some others... "....to an Ohio tourist?" HOLYTOLEDO "...to a seamstress?" ILLBEDARNED "...to a teetotaler?" WELLINEVER "...to a Thanksgiving guest?" GOODGRAVY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/1/2017&g=25&d=A ....

I solved this in less than half my average time…something I have to say I owe to my wife, who was a horticulturist and who still starts who-knows-how-many plants under grow lights in winter, before moving them out to her small greenhouse, and with whom I watch gardening shows so she can get ideas for our (well, her) gardens. To save myself embarrassment, I’ve had to learn flower names so we don’t keep having conversations like:

Me: “I like that flower there.”
Her: “Which one?”
Me: “That blue one.”
Her: “Which blue one? There are three different plants with blue flowers.”

Etc. Etc. Etc.

Clever, fun puzzle. I am far from a botanist but the crosses provided the little extra I needed to complete the fill.

Rating:
8/10
Nicely done and congratulations, James!
the birds and the bees and the flowers and the trees https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n-n-BeG4txo

I enjoyed this one a lot. I came here to point out that TOMATO blossoms are buzz pollinated by bees (watch a video) and here in Southern California one of our iconic spring wildflowers, which makes excellent honey, is black SAGE.

Nice theme although it reminded me of the weekend I spent this last week at the er with my cat after she got into daylilys.  Owning a cat is an impromptu introduction into botany whether you want it or not.

In the good perspective, my cat is happy and I missed s pr today by only 20 seconds!  Nice puzzle!
Great Scott! FITZGERALD fit, and I was so pleased with that.  Oh well.  Fun puzzle, if a little too easy for Wednesday.
Excellent job James! There are some real bloomers in this bouquet of a puzzle – well done.

I briefly thought the "DC good guy" was the BAgMAN, who delivers the bribes.

You're a DAISY if you do.

Watching Wisteria bloom as I solve this garden of earthly delights.

Thank you James

A nod to the pollinators, who are busy these days. Blooming in our garden now: Jerusalem sage, Mystic Spires salvia, African daisies, Mexican bush sage, autumn sage, thryallis, rose mallow, and several native and hybrid lantanas.
I sigh audibly every time I see "master of ceremonies" (m.c.) written out as "emcee."

My only dispensary visit was in Cincinnati, about two years ago. I got the distinct impression that their primary product was likely not CBD. That snake oil is readily available at many, if not all, tobacco shops. I left empty-handed, which was exactly my plan. My “flower” song: https://youtu.be/Qp3eFAKgbDE?t=7&si=ShO2eQKU76MhpvC_

Plants, are my favorite people, lol.

Fun puzzle, loved the clues in the NW corner, clever misdirections.

I finished this early this AM, because it's raining, leaving me sitting inside with my coffee, otherwise I'd be out in the garden, dodging all the stingy (hard 'g') things and checking for the sneaky bindweed that oozes through the fence from my less gardening-inclined neighbor's yard. 

The blooms do glow under the overcast sky though, and the pansies and the iris appreciate a cooler day, and a gentle shower.
Have a good one all.

I was thrown off by 19A: They're found in a circle. A circle has only one radius, so I dismissed my initial thought, only to find that it was the correct answer. Should the clue not have read: They're found in circles?

Overheard in a honkey-tonk in MEMPHIS  - HAMM and TOMATO on RYE? SMH, got my PBJ and INTRIGUE CROSSPOLLINATED.  Here we go:

HALT! Who grows there? 
It's PEONY me. 
METOO! 
I done had y'all in my RADII. Yeah, you and your purdy TOMATO. 
Leave AZALEA out of it, I love her DEARLY. 
Hand over the MOOLA, or I'll plant you two!
AWE, PETE, OCCAM y'all gotta mess with us. Ain't we PALS? 
You got a HELLA nerve. Go on, DENY it. You two been trying to RESALE double-licked BLUEBELL pints. 
That's a LIE! DRAW and get ready to meet your REAPER, 
(N.B.; I'm no shrinking VIOLET, but still readers, get BRACED for impact) 
Help! I done been shot in my HIBISCUS!
 ETUDE, Then fall, bad ICE cream. 
HOLYTOLEDO, I MIST! 

"Th-th-th-that's OWL, folks!" 
We'll MEAT again, dear solvers as EVER, W.N.
22A, "Late spring or early summer bloom" I knew would confound me, since in Texas some flowers bloom in December.

I like a  botanical theme, but don't think any of these pairs have a chance of cross-pollinating (productively), no matter how many BEES visit them. 

Some interesting long, non-thematic entries, which helped me enjoy this puzzle despite it's somewhat wonky theme.

Overheard in a honkey-tonk in MEMPHIS . . .
HAMM and TOMATO on RYE?  . . . 

SMH . . . got my PBJ and INTRIGUE CROSSPOLLINATED . . . . here we go . . .


HALT!

Who grows there?

It's PEONY me.

METOO! 

I done had y'all in my RADII. Yeah, you and your purdy TOMATO.

Leave AZALEA out of it, I love her DEARLY.

Hand over the MOOLA, or I'll plant you two . . .

AWE, PETE, OCCAM y'all gotta mess with us. Ain't we PALS? 

You got a HELLA nerve. Go on, DENY it. You two been trying to RESALE double-licked BLUEBELL pints.

That's a LIE!

DRAW and get ready to meet your REAPER, 

(N.B.; I'm no shrinking VIOLET, but still readers, get BRACED for impact . . .)

OH lordy, I done been shot in my HIBISCUS!

ETUDE, Then fall, bad ICE cream.

HOLYTOLEDO, I MIST!

Th-th-th-that's OWL, folks!"

We'll MEAT again, dear solvers
as EVER,
W.N.

Well, I must admit I came to complain about the vagueness of a lot of the clues, and that so many strange words had to be used to make the theme work, but I am now being informed that this puzzle was in fact “too easy” and “Monday difficulty” by mustachio-twirling crosswordologists.

I must also admit, in my shame, that i revealed the entire puzzle out of frustration. I’m not enough of a mind-reader for some of these clues.

BUT, as a frequent solver of Mondays and Tuesdays, this was far from a Monday. Mondays are like “The color of a ripe banana” solving to YELLOW, or “Month after April” is MAY.

“Viper, etc” equals CAR. Come on…

This was fun, especially because I love gardening and it was a challenge as well for me. I just started doing Wednesday puzzles a month or so ago, and they're still a bit difficult, but I enjoyed this one a lot.

Just curious if others have difficulty figuring out the vertical words. If they're short, It's usually fine, but if they're longer, I invariably have to write it out on paper with blanks. And as soon as I do, It pops into my head.

What's this? Another EMCEE? But I know it now. Lovely crossword, I did like the flowers. Keen gardener.

I had gin for a Manhattan, not knowing any better and RYE isn't one that springs to mind for me, and that held me up until I knew it had to be EMCEE. I've only met a dispensary in a hospital, other kinds are not available here. I wish.
A fun, springy theme. Didn't quite feel seasonally appropriate out here in Colorado, where we've gotten more snow last night than we did the rest of this past "winter."
Is anyone else having an easy week? So far, I'm solving in just under half my average time. I'm sure I've jinxed myself for the rest of the week now!
Hmm? A lei made from the flower hibiscus?  Has anyone see one? Plumeria, orchid, pikake yes. Hibiscus no.

Enjoyed this crossword puzzle. Very clever with the use of flowers especially since we’re all ready to see the blossoms. Living in Hawaii in the 60s and 70s I saw many leis - orchid, plumeria, pikake but never saw a lei made from hibiscus. Hmm? Hibiscus is a large flower for a lei. A better clue might’ve been - Hawaii state flower.
I had fun with this one. Hit a wall on the first go round last night and took a second look at it today. The NE and SE were sticky but came together with some care and attention.

“soccer” in the across clues! Shout out to my club FTFC… Don’t Be Late!

a/ soccer is all
soccer is movement
deciding merit
touching victory
soccer is path stop intersect margin path
circled path found
soccer is right and good and
soccer is the club
d/ a family found
in the music of play
in the soul of flair
in the winning a/ cross
for ever in awe
Peru is not the country closest to the equator without touching it.

The New York Times published an article yesterday all about ([6D Bed cover]) MULCHing. It's called "the Right Way to Mulch." Lots of good ideas. But I agree with the author who discourages the use of landscape fabric. (It's not a good thing.) https://www.nytimes.com/2026/05/05/realestate/mulch-gardening-tips-trees.html?searchResultPosition=1

Okay, everyone-- Apparently, the kerfuffle of the day is whether Hibiscus flowers, live or fake, can be used in leis. Here's what we know so far-- 1) Although the internet can provide plenty of FAKE NEWS otherwise, let us defer to the observations of residents, current and former, of Hawaii. 2) OTOH, dear @MOL, the Hibiscus, in particular the Yellow Hibiscus (*Hibiscus brackenridgei*), is--at least nowadays--the official State Flower of Hawaii--it's endangered, so don't pick it: https://dlnr.hawaii.gov/ecosystems/rare-plants/species/ma%CA%BBo-hau-hele/ 3) *Hibiscus* is a genus within the Mallow family, *Malvaceae.* The family contains another, related genus, *Althæa*. The common garden plant Rose-of-Sharon is often called "Althea," but is currently placed in the genus *Hibiscus*: *Hibiscus syriacus.* Why? Go ask the botanists. 4) Back to mallows--many mallow species like to grow in marshy ground--the marsh mallows. The root of one species of marsh mallow, *Althæa officinalus*, had medicinal uses as a cough suppressant (etc.). It also had thickening properties, and French apothecaries/confectioners (the professions often overlapped) would mix it with beaten egg whites and sugar it to create a confection called "pâte de guimauve." Later, the marsh mallow root was replaced with gelatin, but the name ("marshmallow") stuck. 5) Don't try making Rice Krispy Treats from stale-dated marshmallows, as my partner discovered yesterday. They, too, stuck. Aren't you glad you asked?
Beat my average, but this one had me going in circles a bit. Had HYACINTH instead of HIBISCUS, serves me right for having a Black Thumb.
William of Ockham, not Occam.

I thought this all fit together very nicely! Liked SOCRATIC for method of teaching and CBD was my last to fall. It’s been almost nine years since
my husband(a doctor of the weed) transitioned.
He did other things, too! My current beau partakes a bit but my head isn’t as…all that into it!
Shout out to my friends’ cat MEMPHIS who passed away last week! He will be missed.
A little easy for a Wednesday but very satisfying.

I was not, as they say, on the constructors wavelength. I must have nearly doubled my time trying to fix all the wrong answers I had after the first pass. I was putting in simple answers for the wordplay clues and “clever” answers for ones that turned out to be straightforward.
It didn’t help that I know absolutely nothing about flowers.

Hi all, James here!

Thank you to everyone who tackled my puzzle today, shared it with loved ones, and left thoughtful comments. I was actually able to surprise my family with this debut in person (they also struggled a bit with that NW corner!). The response from this community has been so exciting. What an experience.

I've been reading every comment... Genuinely touched by the stories of your personal connections to the theme and the fill.

A few extra comments:
- @Bill : The ETUDE clue was not mine (I prefer the new clue, it's a great tidbit - thanks to Will Shortz and the editing team)
- @Alex : PERU was my clue (love a little trivia) and thanks! I think the lack of Americana in the puzzle may 'stem' from my Canadian 'roots!'
- @The X-Phile : Beautiful story
- @Everyone : I did not conspire with Ian about MIA HAMM in the midi (coincidence!). Glad you like the 'theme-adjacent' TOMATO/SAGE/RYE/MULCH... I also tried sneaking in the Carolina REAPER as my clue but the harvester works, too!

Have a great week, everybody!

-James

I’d love to own a Viper, but a Challenger R/T would be my preference. I believe 2023 was the last year for the true V8 Dodge Challenger. Stepping up to the Hellcat or Charger SRT would attract lots of attention in these parts.
You see, the 2023 and earlier muscle cars around here are mostly owned by the Arab convenience store cartel and many young Hispanics in the restaurant business. Then, there are those in the wholesale business pushing “product,” who own quite the share of Mustang, Hellcat and Camaro, while we old guys go for the Corvette Stingray. Hmmm… should I?

If you ever find yourself in Memphis, make sure to add the Stax Museum to your itinerary. While it may not be the original location of Stax Records, it does house a limited collection of original equipment and instruments. The merchandise is quite impressive, although it’s quite pricey. I may just change my Wordplay icon to their logo, just for grins.

Speaking of museums, if you ever visit the Space Coast (Melbourne, Fl) definitely give the National Navy UDT-SEAL Museum a visit. Just… wow!

Wow I read that a group called Defending Education got the US Dept of Education to target
Smith College for admitting trans women. I can’t
think of a worse misnomer for such a group than
“Defending Education”.

What he gave to you and what you gave to him are what the humanities are all about.  He taught you to see and know about what you were seeing, and you took the trouble find him to thank him for something that neither of you could have predicted while you were taking the class: the profound gift of of enriching common, everyday experiences for a lifetime.

I loved this puzzle! I am a relatively new solver but find many Wednesday puzzles fairly easy - this one was pleasantly challenging and a bit of a head  scratcher at times. I am an avid gardener so got all the flowers fairly quickly, but mostly from a couple crosses rather than some of the esoteric clues. Very well done, constructor, thank you!

Oh my gosh, I adored this puzzle! And the constructor notes? All the joy, right here. It's becoming a much too common occurrence for me that I only manage to comment long after I finished my solve — but thankfully at the same time there have been puzzles like this one worth persevering to do so! There's some lovely, gently Wednesday-level wordplay like 1D, 2D, 6D... and for good measure, 28A and 43A. Beautiful inclusion of all the nature — and nature-adjacent — fill scattered throughout. Even CAMO, as it is clued! A reminder of its non-pedestrian origins and important intention. I loved the [Viper] pair, the [Airdrops], and Alice, and Prince! As clued, that Purple (VIOLET?) Rain (MIST?) earworm was a foregone conclusion. Thank you, James, for this doubling down on Spring. It seems to me you exhibit wonderful ÉLAN in your debut grid, and I can't wait for more! Aforementioned foregone conclusion, to conclude: https://youtu.be/aXJhDltzYVQ?si=ciryElepqSc0PIu7

@Francis
So this is just like moving house. You're still you but all your landmarks must change. You have to figure out the best short cut to the mall; decide whether the store with the best lettuce is worth the extra 45c/head; look for the most comfortable bench midway on the long walk to the library. It'll all get sorted but just during this transition period, you'll feel lost-some. Keep your eye on the horizon and keep walking. You'll find your range again.

@Francis

SLEEPY.

If you still don't see it, here's a hint. "Grumpy" has an obscured capital letter.

@Francis

The puzzles are harder, partly because they reference things from ten years ago. But I also think some of the construction isn't as good. At times when faced with a sea of unknowns and uninferables I turn on Autocheck, because the puzzle has become something to be done with rather than something to savor. Perhaps we both need a break.

@Chris

ELAN (élan) is a French word that means....[Stylish flair]. But it's more of a thing: it's a whole way of classiness (?).

From the internet:
"Élan refers to energetic spirit, style, and enthusiastic vivacity, commonly used to describe a flair for life or performance. Top synonyms include panache, verve, brio, dash, flair, vivacity, spirit, and animation. It embodies a combination of high energy, confidence, and stylish elegance."

@Chris
ELAN = flair
"Stylish" is just an adjective modifying "flair" (somewhat superfluously, IMHO)

Hope that helps.

@lucky13
Much better than my input; I should have read all the rest before putting in my few cents'-worth.

@Chris
the PEONies in the heather garden in Ft. Tryon park are getting ready to pop and I can't wait. So... in our biome I'd say they're a mid-late spring bloom. At Arnold Arboretum they have probably already started, and should peak in late May.

@lucky13
Audrey Hepburn in the second half of "Funny Face".

@Bob T. Yes, PEONies are very beautiful and dramatic flowers. They are a perennial, so they come back every year. However, you might not see them at all in the winter; they completely die back to the ground. Then they send their reddish stalks up. Mine in New York City are just starting to show buds. When they bloom, they look like huge fluffy roses. The flowers are heavy and the bushy stalks will droop down with the weight. They come in a variety of colors like pink and white. I believe that peonies figure prominently in Asian art, including on pottery, etc. I've read that peony plants are very long-lived. One plant can live for 35 years. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TJ4YK0iLuDQ

@Cat Lady Margaret

OCCAM's PEONY:  Hope you like ants.

OCCAM's OCHO:  How the pesky squirrels eat 8x the sunflower seeds you put out for the songbirds.

@TravisS
This is an odd nit to pick.

There is, of course, a rule that a word can't be used twice in the same crossword. But repeating a word in two different crosswords? Why would that be an issue? Do these two puzzles even have the same editor?

The idea that constructors are somehow "copying" each other is ridiculous. How could the constructor of today's crossword possibly have copied today's Midi? Did James Mattina ride in a time machine to today's date, see that the Midi would mention Mia HAMM, and then go back and put Mia HAMM in his own crossword? Or is it more likely that it's mere coincidence? This sounds like a job for OCCAM's Razor.

@Andrzej

Trees, pretty much the same.  And bushes.  

Really all plant life bores me to tears.  Can I say things like that out loud?

commentBody:

Oh and I know tulips from the Netherlands and sunflowers from Van Gogh and dandelions from blowing their premature forms to smithereens and eating their adult forms when I was a child.

And a maple tree from hockey and a weed plant from college and poison ivy and sumac from an abortive and brief sojourn among the boy scouts.

It doesn't help that the few times I have tried to grow plants or tend them for others I inevitably murder them.

commentBody:
@Francis
The short answer is:  No.
But, be honest:  don't you look at a natural scene--say, at a park--and think it's lovely?

To me, not recognizing different trees, shrubs, vines, flowers, is akin to not recognizing one's children.  Difficult to conceive... but, yes, DHubby once walked past his son without recognizing him (after a week at camp.)  I've tried to point out, for example, the difference between dogwood and redbud (even when not in bloom) --which is very obvious--and he misses most of the time.  Sigh.

@Mean Old Lady

I was hoping to get a reply just like this from someone.

Yes, I know.  Somehow I'm failing to extract all the pleasure that life has to offer, because I know people who are transported by the beauty and mystery of plant life.  

I can sometimes get a peek into this facet of life, but something prevents me from enjoying them to the fullest.

This is like a lot of things we've discussed on this forum.  For example the love of math.  I really wish I could get the same feeling of awe from plants as I get from math.

@Andrzej

I love nature, too.  But I'm way too attracted by creature comforts to really fully explore nature.  I haven't been camping since I was a kid.  (Nor have I taken my sons camping, which is a crime in some circles.)  

Anyway, I read that story when I was not too much older than Ralph or Jack or Simon or "Piggy".  I knew from the first pages how it was going to descend into madness, because that was my experience with children, myself included, left to their own devices.

Then to realize that adults aren't really much better?  Pure horror.

@SP

Should’ve waited one more minute! 😁
@Mike Those questions were planted!

@Mike
Sharp as a pistil, as usual.  You sepal, there's no stigma attached; glad you're stamen the course.
@Mike I'm glad to hear that you were able to cholorophyll that one.

@Mike
Be sure to stem your excitement and cultivate further growth.  Scores like these should be cherished!  Be sure to phylum in the right place.

commentBody:

Gimme for me and probably many others. Apparently your mileage varies. And to my mind, better than another college or university abbreviation clue.

@Bill Completely obvious to me - I think this one might be generational.
@Stacey I’m old but I knew…just because of my daughter….sometimes I know thanks to the grandkids.

@Stacey I’m 73 and got it immediately…but I have to add that my two daughters played Spice Girls quite a lot when they were young (they’re in their 40s now). Often when I do the crossword I’m grateful that I paid attention to the popular culture my sons and daughters liked when they were growing up.

@Stacey
I was in my late 30s with no tween/teen girls in my life when they burst onto the scene in the 1990s, and as much as I tried to ignore then, they've hung in there as a pop culture force as a group and then individually all this time.  I will admit to coming to  enjoying their first song and it's video at a fairly late date.

@Andrzej
I heartily disagree.

"Dispensary inits.," to me, mean CCs.  "The Dispensary" is where you went for immunizations, for minor injuries or illnesses, and for medications, which as dispensed, usually, in amounts known as CCs.  (Army kid.)

I have no more knowledge (or interest) in Spice Girls than in alchemy or Biblical Inerrancy.  And now I find out that I've finished with A Wrong Letter.  ....without understanding what CBD is.  Neither the clue nor the initials mean anything to be, apparently.  Phooey.  I don't mind the Fail (I recently broke my Wordle streak of 212, too,) but I hate to blow it over something stupid or forgettable.  And yet, here I am.  I thnk I'll go back and reread X-Phile's post.

@Bill

I believe that MELB serves as one of the judges on the ubiquitous talent show (starring Simon Cowell) called "America's Got Talent."

@Andrzej

@Mean Old Lady

@Stacey not generational, but I confess not to know the last name initials of the Spice Girls. That whole little section was hard for me because I didn’t know the text for “I cant even”—for a while I had SMN (shoot me now). Maybe THAT is generational. I sure wouldn’t say it now. Too close to real.

@Mean Old Lady

@The X-Phile Wonderful story. So moving…both that you learned a great deal in a class you took reluctantly, and that you thanked him years later. I am a believer in acknowledging the teachers who have changed our lives. May he rest in peace.
@The X-Phile incredible story. Thank you for sharing

@The X-Phile

Thanks for sharing. As a university professor I found the story especially moving.

@The X-Phile

@MOL

Mr Reilly could have been talking learning about the "fun facts" provided by the clues in relation to their respective flowers. (Leaving aside the HIBISCUS 🌺 .)
@Mean Old Lady I think what the constructor meant is that the flowers were pollinated by the word made up by the crossing (circled) letters -- BEES.

@Mean Old Lady

In my mind, cross pollinate was simply a play on words due to the flowers crossing in the puzzle.  I did not think more deeply beyond that, but certainly can see how someone with more knowledge might.  To your point, I did not mislearn the crossing flowers were actual cross pollinators.

@Mean Old Lady

Everyone throws love at the BEES, but few remember to thank their night-time counterparts, the moths.  Turn off your porch lights, people, the moths need to get to your flowers, not die on your lightbulbs.
@Mean Old Lady you sure are assuming a lot

@Don H Your pro-moth post immediately called to mind the great Don Marquis' poem (well, actually written by archy the cockroach but that's another story), "The Moth". Here it is: https://donmarquis.com/reading-room/moth/ An excellent website to roam.

@MOL I'm glad you confirmed this, because it was my first thought as well. Also, although *Hibiscus* may be the State Flower of Hawaii, the genus is cosmopolitan--even here, around the Great Lakes, the Swamp Rose-mallow, *Hibiscus moscheutos*, can be seen blooming in muddy drainage ditches along the roadsides. It's quite beautiful! https://michiganflora.net/record/1671

@Mean Old Lady

Have you googled "hibiscus lei"? 
There seems to be quite a few entries that call into question your assertion.

It may be that the hibiscuses of Hawaii are made of sterner stuff than the ones in our yards.

@The X-Phile

Hibiscuses? Hibisci? Hibiscopodes?
@Mean Old Lady They use fake hibiscus to make leis and you can buy them on Amazon. Fairly common at graduations.

@Mean Old Lady

My limited knowledge and a couple of crosses enabled me to get HIBISCUS pretty quickly sans outrage.  Won't be the last time, I imagine.

@Mean Old Lady

This comment, and Steve L's amusing reply to my comment, made me look up the etymology of HIBISCUS.
It come from the Greek  hibiskos and apparently referred to the plant we call the "marsh mallow" (Althaea officinalis).

And that reminded me of a lesson from my old Plant Tax teacher.  "Yes, marshmallows were originally made from the sap of the Marsh Mallow.  Although now, they are almost always made with artificial flavorings."
I believe he even broke the stem of one and let us taste the sap.

@Bill
The state flower is LEHUA (or it was when Statehood was announced and celebrated way back when!)

@The X-Phile
For a moment I thought that you were referring to a tax on plants - not even the Beatles thought of that one: 
"I'll tax the street
 If you try to sit, I'll tax your seat"
But it's only the US predilection for abbreviation again.

@lucky13
I responded to someone later....  ALTHEA ("Rose of Sharon") is *related* but that doesn't make the clue correct.  
Blossoms chosen for LEIs are long-lasting and fragrant--both qualities not found in the HIBISCUS.

People here buy HIBISCUS plants in pots and throw them out when winter comes.  Needless to say, they would not survive the winter even though we're in the South.  
When we lived in Hawaii, we had a huge hedge of red HIBISCUS.  It flowered almost continuously, but the flooms last ONLY ONE DAY even on the shrub.  

FAKE (plastic) blossoms don't count (see below) ....

@The X-Phile

My camp counselor had us tasting sassafras root, which was the flavoring for root beer, traditionally.

@Mean Old Lady
Are you seriously this upset?  Much more worrying things in the world. 

And it turns out, your complaint is not even accurate. 

Relax !
@Mean Old Lady My theory is that the writer was thinking of the tradition of wearing a hibiscus flower behind your ear in Hawaii and other Pacific islands, and got that mixed up with lei flowers.
@Mean Old Lady isn’t that funny, when I saw that clue my very first thought was hibiscus. I just associate them in my mind.

@Alexia

That's just MOL's M.O.  — she is the "mean old lady", after all.

And because there are much more worrying things in the world, it's nice to get to focus on more trivial matters for a few moments – clear one's mind from other concerns. 

Relax!

@tricia

Erik,
A circle has an infinite number of RADII (all of equal length).

@Erik A circle has infinite radii. A radius is defined as a line from the midpoint of a circle to the outer edge. You can draw infinite of those of arbetrary angularity. Think of  long minute hand on a huge clock that moves one planck length each planck second. Each of those new positions is a new radius. But it gets even more fine grained than that because planck lenghts are the physical limitation of this reality. In pure theoretical geometry there could be movements even SMALLER than planck lenghts....INFINITELY smaller....leading to an infinite number of radii in a circle! 🤔

@Calvin W 
I think the cool thing about the viper = car is we had already seen viper = asp elsewhere in the puzzle, so we had to stop and think about what sort of ckever misdirection could be going on.

@Calvin W

There has been a decent amount of discussion lately of why longtime solvers may find a puzzle relatively easy, while newcomers struggle mightily with the same puzzle.  I think that to a large extent, it has to do with the ability to recognize polysemy, the variety of meanings any given word has.

In the viper case you cite, here you must stretch your thinking beyond snakes.  In fact, you might notice that the clue for  CAR comes right after the ASPS clue, both with "viper" in it.  One might assume that they both refer to snakes, but an experienced solver might think that, since "viper" was used in reference to snakes once, it has to do with something else here.

It is not immediately obvious to everyone that a viper can be anything but a snake, but seasoned cruciverbalists know to think beyond the obvious.  They figure out CAR with so little difficulty that they don't even notice that it's a tricky clue.  (Of course, it depends on your life experiences.  Some NYC residents, lacking car culture, might not even know that there's such a car.)

Also, experienced solvers get many crosses easily.  Any one of the three crosses, OCCAM, SOCRATIC and PRIMROSE, are easy gets for experienced solvers. So they may even get CAR without ever looking at it.

How long does it take to get to this point?  Well, I've been solving for over 40 years, but with all the tools that are available these days (like Wordplay), it shouldn't take newer solvers anywhere as long.  

Happy solving!

@Calvin W
Dodge hasn't produced the Viper since 2017, and NYCers take perverse pride in not knowing anything about CARs.

@Steve L

Very well said! 

When I started out, some 5 years ago, I was – excuse the pun – clueless. Even Mondays had misdirects that boggled my mind.

Aside from reading every article on how to solve the NYT xwords, and lurking in these comments to absorb explanations from the veterans such as yourself, I relied on auto-check to reverse engineer the rationale of the answers.

Analyzing the clue [viper] being ASP, followed by it being CAR, gave me the needed aha of parallel thinking, and I've come to love misdirects. 

When I went off the crutches of auto-check, Mondays would take me over half an hour to unravel! It was a fabulous exercise in patience, commitment, and humble expectations.
If a true beginner aces the solve without breaking a sweat, either he/she is in fact a genius – or the gold standard has turned to brass.

@Grant I mean, are you saying that a Cadillac El Dorado or a Ford Focus are not cars because they are no longer in production? Are you also saying that every NYT crossword must be specifically tailored to the general knowledge of New Yorkers?

I think the ASPS/CAR clues being so close together was almost an inside joke from the designer to the moderately experienced solver who has see ASP clued as viper a million times. Cluing it as Viper with the uppercase V means it is a proper noun and pretty apparent that some other use of the word is in effect.

From no less august a source than hawaiianencyclopedia.com: 

"The Turk's cap hibiscus reaches heights of about 13 feet (4 m), with bright red, tubular flowers that are about 2 to 2-1/2 inches (5 to 6 cm) long with protruding stamens.  Several varieties of the pendulous flowers are cultivated in the Hawaiian Islands, where they blossom year round, though not as often from December to February.
The blossoms of the Turk's cap never fully open, and so they do not wilt as easily as other hibiscus flowers.  This makes them ideal for use in lei, which are often assembled Micronesian style, tied or woven into a flat collar. (See Lei Making Methods.)  About 90 blossoms are required for a standard 40-inch (100-cm) lei."
